title: Cache Memory — N-Way Set Associative Mapping
examTip: Set Associative is the best-of-both-worlds between Direct Mapping and Fully Associative. The cache is divided into S sets, each containing N lines (N-way). A memory block can go to any line within its mapped set. Address = Tag | Set Index | Word Offset. HIT: find the set using Set Index, then compare tag against ALL N lines in that set using N comparators in parallel. Most modern CPUs use 4-way or 8-way set associative L1/L2 caches.

blocks.label: N-Way Set Associative Cache — 2-Way Example (4 Sets, 2 Ways)
TAG (30 bits)
blocks.note: Compared against BOTH ways in the mapped set

blocks.label: Set Index = Block# mod (Number of Sets S)    |    Block can go to ANY line within its Set
CACHE (2-Way Set Associative: 4 Sets × 2 Ways = 8 Lines Total)
Cache Lookup Process (on every memory access)
Step 1: Extract Set Index from address → select the set row
Step 2: Compare address TAG against Way-0 tag AND Way-1 tag simultaneously (parallel comparators)
HIT ✅: A tag matches → MUX selects the matching way → return data immediately
MISS ❌: No tag matches → fetch block from memory → place in set using LRU/FIFO replacement
Comparison: Direct vs Set Associative vs Fully Associative
sublabels: Direct Mapped (1-way): 1 comparator, fastest hardware, highest conflict miss rate.
N-way Set Associative: N comparators per set, N-way freedom within set — balances speed and miss rate. Used in modern CPUs (L1=4-way, L2=8-way, L3=16-way).
Fully Associative: C comparators (one per line), no conflict misses, most expensive hardware. Used in TLBs.

theory:
N-Way Set Associative Mapping combines the advantages of Direct Mapping (hardware simplicity) and Fully Associative Mapping (flexibility). The cache is divided into S sets, with each set containing N lines (called 'ways'). A memory block maps to a specific set (using modulo), but within that set it can occupy ANY of the N lines.

Address Structure: Tag | Set Index | Word Offset
- Set Index bits: log₂(S), directly selects the set.
- Tag bits: compared simultaneously against all N ways in the selected set.

Lookup Process:
1. Use Set Index bits to select the set.
2. In parallel, compare the address tag with all N tag registers in that set.
3. If any comparator matches AND Valid=1 → HIT → MUX selects that way's data.
4. If no comparator matches → MISS → fetch from memory, use replacement policy (LRU/FIFO).

Replacement Policies (on miss when set is full):
- LRU (Least Recently Used): Replace the way not accessed for the longest time. Best practical performance.
- FIFO (First In, First Out): Replace the oldest loaded line.
- Random: Simple, avoids worst-case patterns.

Industry Usage:
- L1 Cache: typically 4-way set associative.
- L2 Cache: typically 8-way set associative.
- L3 Cache: typically 16-way set associative.
- TLB: typically Fully Associative (small, high-priority).
keyPoints: Cache divided into S sets × N ways; total lines = S × N.
Block maps to Set = Block# mod S; within the set, any of the N ways can hold the block.
Address fields: Tag | Set Index (log₂S bits) | Word Offset.
N parallel comparators check all N tags in the selected set simultaneously.
HIT: tag match + valid=1 in any way. MISS: no match → fetch from memory → LRU/FIFO replacement.
4-way and 8-way are the industry standard for L1/L2 caches in modern CPUs.
useCases: L1, L2, L3 cache design in modern CPUs (Intel, AMD, ARM).
Cache performance analysis — calculating hit rate with set associativity.
Exam problems: calculating number of sets, tag/index/offset bits, LRU replacement sequences.
